config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters deeply, but it should not automatically come before individual rights, because rights are what protect people from abuse by the majority or by the state. From my perspective, a society is healthiest when it balances the common good with strong protections for personal freedom, dignity, and fairness. In Indonesia, I have seen how collective values can build solidarity, but I also think they can become harmful if they are used to silence individuals.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. In the United States, the whole point of constitutional rights is to protect people from being steamrolled by majorities, institutions, or the government when the “greater good” is used as a justification. That said, individual rights are not a license to ignore public safety or basic responsibility, so there are real limits when one person’s actions directly harm others. But once society gets to decide that rights can be overridden whenever it finds it convenient, I think that opens the door to abuse very quickly.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement because putting society above the individual too easily becomes an excuse for abuse, especially by governments or local majorities. From an Indonesian perspective, I value gotong royong and social harmony, but I have also seen how pressure to conform can silence people, excuse corruption, or shame those who are different. Society should protect the common good, yes, but not by treating individual rights as disposable whenever they are inconvenient. A healthy society is one that asks individuals to be responsible while still defending their dignity, freedom, and fair treatment.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From an American perspective, that principle is exactly how you end up justifying censorship, discrimination, or government overreach in the name of the “common good,” and history gives plenty of examples of that going wrong. I do think individual rights come with responsibilities, and there are narrow cases where limits are justified for direct harm, public safety, or due process. But the default should be that society serves people, not that people are expected to surrender their rights whenever the majority says it is convenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From my Indonesian perspective, I value gotong royong and social harmony, but I have also seen how “for the good of society” can become a cover for silencing dissent, pressuring minorities, or excusing unfair treatment. The common good matters, but it is not legitimate if it is built by trampling personal dignity, freedom, and due process. A healthy society protects both: it asks people to contribute, but it also restrains the majority and the state from abusing power.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From an American perspective, I am very wary of any argument that says the common good can override rights too easily, because that is exactly how censorship, discrimination, and government abuse get rationalized. At the same time, I do not treat individual rights as absolute in every situation, because public safety and direct harm to others do justify some limits. But the burden should always be on society to prove that a restriction is truly necessary, not on individuals to constantly justify why their rights still matter.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
